1: diḇrê nᵉḥemyāh ben-ḥaḵalyāh: 'the words of Nehemiah son of Hacaliah.' Nᵉḥemyāh means 'YHWH has comforted' (from nāḥam, to comfort), a theologically resonant name given his role in restoring the exilic community. The memoir opens with a date: the month kislew (November–December) in the twentieth year of Artaxerxes I, placing the scene in 445 BCE. The location is haššûšān habîrāh, 'Susa the citadel' — bîrāh is an Aramaic loanword (cf. Ezra 6:2; Dan 8:2) meaning fortified palace or citadel, reflecting the heavy Aramaic influence on official Persian-period vocabulary. Susa was one of the four Achaemenid capitals (Pasargadae, Ecbatana, Persepolis, Susa); it served as the administrative center where Nehemiah held his court position.
2: wayyāḇôʾ ḥanānî ʾeḥāḏ mēʾaḥay hûʾ waʾanāšîm mîyhûḏāh: 'and Hanani, one of my brothers, came, he and certain men from Judah.' The word ʾāḥ can mean biological brother or a fellow Israelite; Neh 7:2 identifies Hanani as placed over Jerusalem, suggesting a close family relationship. The visitors brought news of conditions in Jerusalem. The phrase hayyᵉhûḏîm happālêṭāh, 'the Jews, the survivors,' uses pālêṭāh (escaped remnant) — the same term used for those who survived the destruction; it frames the post-exilic community as a remnant rather than a full restoration, maintaining the eschatological tension of the period.
3: hammûr ḥārāʿāh gᵉḏôlāh ûḥerpāh: 'in great trouble and shame.' The two terms define the community's condition: rāʿāh is general calamity or evil, and ḥerpāh is the specific shame-word — reproach, disgrace, the social humiliation of a people who have failed to reconstitute their defenses. The walls of Jerusalem were broken (pᵉrûṣāh, the same root used of breaches) and the gates burned with fire. The physical desolation is also covenantal: a city without walls is a city without honor, without standing before other nations, a living embodiment of the curse of Deuteronomy 28.
4: wāʾeḇkeh wāʾetʾabbᵉlāh yāmîm: 'and I wept and mourned for days.' The sequence — weeping, mourning, fasting, and praying — follows the classic lament pattern of the post-exilic community (cf. Dan 9:3; Ezra 9:3-5). ʾāḇal (to mourn) is the formal mourning posture, not mere sadness. Nehemiah's fasting and prayer before the God of heaven (ʾĕlōhê haššāmayim) is the typical Achaemenid-period designation used in official contexts with non-Israelites (cf. Ezra 1:2; Gen 24:3) — it is the ecumenically intelligible name for YHWH in a Persian world.
5: ʾānnāʾ YHWH ʾĕlōhê haššāmayim hāʾēl haggāḏôl wᵉhannôrāʾ šōmēr habᵉrît wāḥeseḏ: 'Please, YHWH God of heaven, the great and awesome God who keeps covenant and steadfast love.' This prayer address is virtually identical to Daniel's prayer in Dan 9:4, both drawing on Deuteronomy's covenant formulary (Deut 7:9, 21). The combination of gāḏôl wᵉnôrāʾ (great and awesome) is the Deuteronomic description of YHWH's character (Deut 10:17); šōmēr habᵉrît wāḥeseḏ (keeping covenant and steadfast love) is the basis for all petition. The prayer's authority rests entirely on what YHWH has already committed to in the covenant — Nehemiah is not making a new appeal but calling in an existing obligation.
6: tᵉhî nāʾ ʾoznᵉḵā qaššeḇet wᵉʿêneḵā pᵉtuḥôt lišmōaʿ: 'let your ear be attentive and your eyes open.' The two anatomical metaphors — ear attentive (qaššeḇet) and eyes open — frame YHWH as a king who can choose to notice or ignore. The prayer is a plea for divine attention. mōḏeh ʾanî ʿal-ḥaṭṭōʾt bᵉnê yiśrāʾēl: 'I confess the sins of the people of Israel' — Nehemiah identifies himself with the community's sin using yāḏāh in the hithpael (confess, acknowledge), the same root used in psalms of lament and corporate confession. The 'we' of confession includes Nehemiah's own household: ûḇêt-ʾāḇî ḥāṭāʾnû, 'I and my father's house have sinned.'
7: ḥāḇôl ḥāḇalnû lāḵ: 'we have acted very corruptly against you.' The infinitive absolute intensifies: literally 'corrupting we have corrupted.' ḥāḇal in the hiphil means to act destructively or corruptly; it frames the community's sin as willful covenant breach. The specific charge: they did not keep (šāmar) the commandments, statutes (ḥuqqîm), and rules (mišpāṭîm) that YHWH commanded Moses. This tripartite legal formula (commandments/statutes/rules) is the standard Deuteronomic covenant language (Deut 4:1, 5, 14; 6:1), signaling that Nehemiah's prayer operates entirely within the Mosaic covenant framework.
8: zᵉḵār-nāʾ ʾet-haddāḇār ʾašer ṣiwwîtā ʾet-mōšeh ʿaḇdeḵā lēʾmōr: 'remember the word you commanded Moses your servant, saying.' Nehemiah quotes Moses' scatter-gather promise (Deut 30:1-5; cf. Lev 26:33): if unfaithful, YHWH will scatter (pûṣ) the people among the nations — but if they return (šûḇ) to YHWH, he will gather them (qāḇaṣ) even from the ends of heaven. ʾim-timdû is conditional: 'if you are faithless.' The prayer is Scripture-informed — Nehemiah is not improvising petition but reminding YHWH of his own declared covenant terms, a legitimate covenantal argument pattern throughout the Psalms and prophets.
9: wᵉšāmtem waʿăśîtem: 'and return and do.' The gather-promise is activated by repentance: šûḇtem (return, turn) is the Deuteronomic repentance verb. The promise: wᵉqibbāṣtîm, 'I will gather them.' The specific destination phrase ʾel-hammāqôm ʾašer-bāḥartî lᵉšakkēn šemî šām, 'to the place I have chosen to cause my name to dwell there,' is the Deuteronomic temple formula (Deut 12:5, 11; 14:23) — the place is Jerusalem and its temple. The prayer grounds the whole restoration project in the Deuteronomic promise of return.
10: wᵉhēm ʿăḇāḏeḵā wᵉʿammᵉḵā ʾašer pādîtā bᵉkōḥᵃḵā haggāḏôl ûbᵉyāḏᵉḵā haḥăzāqāh: 'they are your servants and your people whom you redeemed by your great power and by your strong hand.' pādîtā (you redeemed) is the exodus vocabulary — pādāh is one of the primary Hebrew words for redemption, used throughout Deuteronomy for YHWH's act of purchasing Israel from Egypt (Deut 7:8; 9:26; 15:15). By invoking the exodus redemption, Nehemiah grounds his petition in YHWH's prior, defining act of commitment to Israel — the exile cannot be the final word because YHWH already proved his redemptive will at the exodus.
11: ʾānnāʾ ʾăḏōnāy tᵉhî nāʾ ʾoznᵉḵā qaššeḇet: 'O Lord, let your ear be attentive.' The prayer concludes with renewed petition: grant success (haṣlaḥ-nāʾ) and mercy (lᵉraḥămîm) before Artaxerxes. The closing note is key: waʾanî hāyîtî mašqeh lammeleḵ, 'and I was the king's cupbearer.' mašqeh (cupbearer, literally 'one who gives to drink') was a position of intimate royal trust — the cupbearer tasted the king's wine to detect poison and therefore had unsupervised access to the royal presence. The Genesis parallel (Gen 40-41) shows the long biblical history of Israelites serving in this role; Nehemiah's position is what makes the prayer for favor with the king theologically significant: he already has access that most Israelites could never obtain.
